1236	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — D1: the presheaf pullback Lan decomposition
1237	
1238	These are general `PresheafOfModules` statements (any `F : C ⥤ D`, ring presheaves
1239	`R, S`), not specific to schemes; they are project-local because Mathlib's pinned commit
1240	exposes neither `extendScalars` nor `pullback₀` at the presheaf-of-modules level. Both are
1241	realised as the left adjoint of an existing right adjoint: `pushforward₀ F R` is
1242	definitionally `pushforward (𝟙 (F.op ⋙ R))` (because `restrictScalars (𝟙) = 𝟭` on the nose,
1243	witnessed by Mathlib's `restrictScalars (𝟙 R)).Full := inferInstanceAs (𝟭 _).Full`), and
1244	`restrictScalars φ` is definitionally `pushforward (F := 𝟭) φ`; since `pushforward _` is
1245	always a right adjoint, so are these two, and their left adjoints `pullback₀`/`extendScalars`
1246	exist. The decomposition `pullback φ ≅ extendScalars φ ⋙ pullback₀` then follows from the
1247	definitional factorisation `pushforward φ = pushforward₀ F R ⋙ restrictScalars φ` by
1248	uniqueness of left adjoints (`Adjunction.leftAdjointCompIso`).
1249	
1250	Per blueprint `lem:pullback_lan_decomposition` (D1). **OFF-PATH (iter-243 pivot).** D1 is
1251	axiom-clean and self-contained, but it was the first brick of the ABANDONED general
1252	strong-monoidal pullback build (`sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality`, route (e)); the active
1253	route is the loc-triv chart-chase D1'–D4' (§below), which does NOT consume `extendScalars`/
1254	`pullback₀`. Retained as a correct, reusable presheaf-level decomposition; do NOT extend it
1255	toward the general build (D2 scalar-strong / D3 topological-interchange are NOT pursued). -/
1256	
1257	section PullbackLanDecomposition
1258	
1259	variable {C : Type u} [Category.{u} C] {D : Type u} [Category.{u} D]
1260	  {F : C ⥤ D} {R : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat.{u}} {S : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat.{u}}
1261	
1262	/-- `pushforward₀ F R` is a right adjoint: it is definitionally `pushforward (𝟙 (F.op ⋙ R))`
1263	(since `restrictScalars (𝟙) = 𝟭` on the nose). Project-local; carries the existence of the
1264	topological inverse image `pullback₀`. -/
1265	private lemma pushforward₀IsRightAdjoint (F : C ⥤ D) (R : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat.{u}) :
1266	    (PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀.{u} F R).IsRightAdjoint :=
1267	  inferInstanceAs (PresheafOfModules.pushforward.{u} (𝟙 (F.op ⋙ R))).IsRightAdjoint
1268	
1269	/-- `restrictScalars φ` is a right adjoint: it is definitionally `pushforward (F := 𝟭) φ`.
1270	Project-local; carries the existence of the extension of scalars `extendScalars`. -/
1271	private lemma restrictScalarsIsRightAdjoint (φ : S ⟶ F.op ⋙ R) :
1272	    (PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars.{u} φ).IsRightAdjoint :=
1273	  inferInstanceAs
1274	    (PresheafOfModules.pushforward.{u} (F := 𝟭 C) (R := F.op ⋙ R) φ).IsRightAdjoint
1275	
1276	/-- **The topological inverse image `pullback₀ := (pushforward₀ F R).leftAdjoint`**, the
1277	left adjoint of the fixed-ring pushforward. On underlying presheaves it is the left Kan
1278	extension along `F.op`. Project-local (no `PresheafOfModules` inverse-image functor at the
1279	pin); the carrier of D3. -/
1280	noncomputable def pullback0 (F : C ⥤ D) (R : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat.{u}) :
1281	    _root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (F.op ⋙ R) ⥤ _root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} R :=
1282	  haveI := pushforward₀IsRightAdjoint F R
1283	  (PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀ F R).leftAdjoint
1284	
1285	/-- **Extension of scalars `extendScalars φ := (restrictScalars φ).leftAdjoint`**, the left
1286	adjoint of restriction of scalars along a morphism of ring presheaves. Project-local; the
1287	carrier of D2 (strong monoidal via `distribBaseChange`). -/
1288	noncomputable def extendScalars (φ : S ⟶ F.op ⋙ R) :
1289	    _root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} S ⥤ _root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (F.op ⋙ R) :=
1290	  haveI := restrictScalarsIsRightAdjoint φ
1291	  (PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars φ).leftAdjoint
1292	
1293	/-- The adjunction `pullback₀ ⊣ pushforward₀`. -/
1294	noncomputable def pullback0Adjunction (F : C ⥤ D) (R : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat.{u}) :
1295	    pullback0 F R ⊣ PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀ F R :=
1296	  haveI := pushforward₀IsRightAdjoint F R
1297	  Adjunction.ofIsRightAdjoint (PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀ F R)
1298	
1299	/-- The adjunction `extendScalars φ ⊣ restrictScalars φ`. -/
1300	noncomputable def extendScalarsAdjunction (φ : S ⟶ F.op ⋙ R) :
1301	    extendScalars φ ⊣ PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars φ :=
1302	  haveI := restrictScalarsIsRightAdjoint φ
1303	  Adjunction.ofIsRightAdjoint (PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars φ)
1304	
1305	/-- **D1 — the presheaf pullback Lan decomposition** (blueprint `lem:pullback_lan_decomposition`).
1306	For `φ : S ⟶ F.op ⋙ R` presenting a pushforward of presheaves of modules, the presheaf
1307	pullback factors as extension of scalars followed by the topological inverse image,
1308	`pullback φ ≅ extendScalars φ ⋙ pullback₀`. This is the left-adjoint reversal of the
1309	definitional factorisation `pushforward φ = pushforward₀ F R ⋙ restrictScalars φ`, obtained
1310	from `Adjunction.leftAdjointCompIso` (uniqueness of left adjoints). Project-local. -/
1311	noncomputable def pullbackLanDecomposition (φ : S ⟶ F.op ⋙ R) :
1312	    PresheafOfModules.pullback φ ≅ extendScalars φ ⋙ pullback0 F R :=
1313	  (Adjunction.leftAdjointCompIso
1314	    (extendScalarsAdjunction φ) (pullback0Adjunction F R)
1315	    (PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction φ)
1316	    (Iso.refl (PresheafOfModules.pushforward φ))).symm
1317	
1318	end PullbackLanDecomposition
1319	
1320	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — D1'–D4' loc-triv pullback–tensor comparison
1321	
1322	The locally-trivial-restricted upgrade of the oplax comparison map
1323	`pullbackTensorMap` (`f^*(M ⊗ N) ⟶ f^*M ⊗ f^*N`) to an isomorphism, blueprint
1324	§`sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality`, sub-lemmas D1'–D4'. -/

1326	section LocTrivPullbackTensor

1328	/-- **The sheafified `⊗ₘ` of the two `pullbackValIso`s (piece 4 of `pullbackTensorMap`) is an
1329	iso.** Factored out as a top-level lemma so the `tensorIso (C := _ ⋙ forget₂)` elaboration mirrors
1330	the proven `tensorObjIsoOfIso` (it does not elaborate cleanly inside a tactic block carrying
1331	`extract_lets` locals). Project-local helper for `isIso_pullbackTensorMap_of_isIso_sheafifyDelta`. -/
1332	private lemma isIso_sheafify_tensorHom_pullbackValIso {X Y : Scheme.{u}}
1333	    (f : Y ⟶ X) (M N : X.Modules) :
1334	    IsIso ((PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := Y.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 Y.ringCatSheaf.val)).map
1335	      (MonoidalCategory.tensorHom
1336	        (C := _root_.PresheafOfModules (Y.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))
1337	        ((SheafOfModules.forget Y.ringCatSheaf).map (pullbackValIso f M).hom)
1338	        ((SheafOfModules.forget Y.ringCatSheaf).map (pullbackValIso f N).hom))) :=
1339	  ((PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := Y.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 Y.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
1340	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorIso
1341	      (C := _root_.PresheafOfModules (Y.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))
1342	      ((SheafOfModules.forget Y.ringCatSheaf).mapIso (pullbackValIso f M))
1343	      ((SheafOfModules.forget Y.ringCatSheaf).mapIso (pullbackValIso f N)))).isIso_hom

1345	/-- **Reduction of `pullbackTensorMap` iso-ness to the sheafified presheaf δ.**
1346	
1347	`pullbackTensorMap f M N` is the four-fold composite
1348	`(sheafificationCompPullback).hom ≫ a_Y.map δ ≫ (sheafifyTensorUnitIso).hom ≫ a_Y.map (tensorHom …)`.
1349	Three of the four factors (`sheafificationCompPullback`, `sheafifyTensorUnitIso`,
1350	and the `tensorHom` of the two `pullbackValIso`s) are isomorphisms unconditionally;
1351	the only conditional factor is the sheafification `a_Y.map δ` of the presheaf-level
1352	oplax comparison `δ`. Hence `pullbackTensorMap f M N` is an iso whenever that
1353	sheafified δ is an iso. This isolates the SOLE remaining content (the sheafified δ)
1354	for D2' (unit pair) and D4' (chart-chase). Project-local. -/
1355	lemma isIso_pullbackTensorMap_of_isIso_sheafifyDelta {X Y : Scheme.{u}}
1356	    (f : Y ⟶ X) (M N : X.Modules)
1357	    (h : letI φ' : (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) ⟶
1358	          (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map f.base).op ⋙ (Y.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) :=
1359	          (f.toRingCatSheafHom).hom
1360	        IsIso ((PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := Y.ringCatSheaf)
1361	          (𝟙 Y.ringCatSheaf.val)).map
1362	          (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.δ (PresheafOfModules.pullback φ') M.val N.val))) :
1363	    IsIso (pullbackTensorMap f M N) := by
1364	  unfold pullbackTensorMap
1365	  extract_lets φ φ' a_Y
1366	  -- piece 2 (the sheafified δ) is the only conditional factor — supplied by `h`.
1367	  haveI hδ : IsIso (a_Y.map
1368	      (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.δ (PresheafOfModules.pullback φ') M.val N.val)) := h
1369	  -- pieces 1 and 3 are `Iso.hom`s; piece 4 is `a_Y.map` of the (iso) `⊗ₘ` of the two
1370	  -- `pullbackValIso`s, supplied by the factored top-level helper.
1371	  exact IsIso.comp_isIso' inferInstance (IsIso.comp_isIso' hδ
1372	    (IsIso.comp_isIso' inferInstance (isIso_sheafify_tensorHom_pullbackValIso f M N)))

1374	/-- **Converse of `isIso_sheafification_map_of_W`.** A morphism of presheaves of modules whose
1375	image under the associated-sheaf functor is an isomorphism lies (on underlying additive presheaves)
1376	in the sheafification localizer `J.W`. This is the same morphism-property identity
1377	`PresheafOfModules.inverseImage_W_toPresheaf_eq_inverseImage_isomorphisms` (the sheafification
1378	functor *is* the localization at `J.W.inverseImage (toPresheaf _)`) read backwards. Project-local:
1379	needed to feed the flatness-free whiskering lemmas `W_whisker{Left,Right}_of_W` from a sheafified
1380	iso (the D2' η-bridge route, below). -/
1381	private lemma W_of_isIso_sheafification {C : Type u} [Category.{u} C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
1382	    {R₀ : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat} {Rsh : CategoryTheory.Sheaf J RingCat} (α : R₀ ⟶ Rsh.obj)
1383	    [Presheaf.IsLocallyInjective J α] [Presheaf.IsLocallySurjective J α]
1384	    [J.WEqualsLocallyBijective AddCommGrpCat] [CategoryTheory.HasWeakSheafify J AddCommGrpCat]
1385	    {A B : _root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} R₀} (f : A ⟶ B)
1386	    (hf : IsIso ((PresheafOfModules.sheafification α).map f)) :
1387	    J.W ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf R₀).map f) := by
1388	  have h := PresheafOfModules.inverseImage_W_toPresheaf_eq_inverseImage_isomorphisms (J := J) α
1389	  have h2 : (CategoryTheory.MorphismProperty.isomorphisms (SheafOfModules Rsh)).inverseImage
1390	      (PresheafOfModules.sheafification α) f := hf
1391	  rw [← h] at h2
1392	  exact h2

1394	/-- **D2' δ-wrapping — the sheafified cotensorator on the unit pair is an iso, given the η-bridge.**
1395	
1396	The presheaf-level oplax unitality `Functor.OplaxMonoidal.left_unitality_hom` factors the
1397	cotensorator `δ (pullback φ') 𝟙_ 𝟙_` of the abstract presheaf pullback through the unit comparison
1398	`η (pullback φ')` (right-whiskered by `F.obj 𝟙_`) and the (iso) left unitor. Sheafifying, the
1399	unitor factor and the `F.map (λ_ 𝟙_)` factor are isomorphisms unconditionally; the whiskered
1400	`η`-factor `a_Y.map (η F ▷ F.obj 𝟙_)` is an iso whenever `a_Y.map (η F)` is — because a sheafified
1401	iso lies in `J.W` (`W_of_isIso_sheafification`), `J.W` is stable under right-whiskering
1402	(`W_whiskerRight_of_W`, flatness-free), and `J.W`-morphisms sheafify to isos
1403	(`isIso_sheafification_map_of_W`). Hence the sheafified `δ` on the unit pair is an iso, which is
1404	exactly the hypothesis the reduction brick `isIso_pullbackTensorMap_of_isIso_sheafifyDelta` consumes
1405	on `M = N = 𝒪`. Project-local; the **δ-wrapping** half of D2' (`lem:pullback_tensor_iso_unit`),
1406	self-contained modulo the η-bridge `IsIso (a_Y.map (η (pullback φ')))`. -/
1407	lemma isIso_sheafifyDelta_unitPair_of_isIso_sheafifyEta {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : Y ⟶ X)
1408	    (h : letI φ' : (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) ⟶
1409	          (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map f.base).op ⋙ (Y.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) :=
1410	          (f.toRingCatSheafHom).hom
1411	        IsIso ((PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := Y.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 Y.ringCatSheaf.val)).map
1412	          (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.η (PresheafOfModules.pullback φ')))) :
1413	    letI φ' : (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) ⟶
1414	        (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map f.base).op ⋙ (Y.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) :=
1415	        (f.toRingCatSheafHom).hom
1416	    IsIso ((PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := Y.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 Y.ringCatSheaf.val)).map
1417	      (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.δ (PresheafOfModules.pullback φ')
1418	        (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf).val (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf).val)) := by
1419	  letI φ' : (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) ⟶
1420	      (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map f.base).op ⋙ (Y.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) :=
1421	      (f.toRingCatSheafHom).hom
1422	  set a_Y := PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := Y.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 Y.ringCatSheaf.val) with ha
1423	  change IsIso (a_Y.map (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.δ (PresheafOfModules.pullback φ') (𝟙_ _) (𝟙_ _)))
1424	  set F := PresheafOfModules.pullback φ' with hF
1425	  have hWη : (Opens.grothendieckTopology (Y : TopCat)).W
1426	      ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf _).map (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.η F)) :=
1427	    W_of_isIso_sheafification (𝟙 Y.ringCatSheaf.val) _ h
1428	  have hWw : (Opens.grothendieckTopology (Y : TopCat)).W
1429	      ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf _).map (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.η F ▷ F.obj (𝟙_ _))) :=
1430	    PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerRight_of_W (R := Y.presheaf) _ _ hWη
1431	  haveI hIsoW : IsIso (a_Y.map (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.η F ▷ F.obj (𝟙_ _))) :=
1432	    PresheafOfModules.isIso_sheafification_map_of_W (𝟙 Y.ringCatSheaf.val) _ hWw
1433	  haveI hIsoLam : IsIso (a_Y.map (λ_ (F.obj (𝟙_ _))).hom) := inferInstance
1434	  have hBC : IsIso (a_Y.map
1435	      (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.η F ▷ F.obj (𝟙_ _) ≫ (λ_ (F.obj (𝟙_ _))).hom)) := by
1436	    rw [Functor.map_comp]; infer_instance
1437	  haveI hD : IsIso (a_Y.map (F.map (λ_ (𝟙_ _)).hom)) := inferInstance
1438	  have hlu := Functor.OplaxMonoidal.left_unitality_hom F (𝟙_ _)
1439	  have key : a_Y.map (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.δ F (𝟙_ _) (𝟙_ _)) ≫
1440	      a_Y.map (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.η F ▷ F.obj (𝟙_ _) ≫ (λ_ (F.obj (𝟙_ _))).hom)
1441	      = a_Y.map (F.map (λ_ (𝟙_ _)).hom) := by
1442	    rw [← Functor.map_comp]; exact congrArg _ hlu
1443	  have heq : a_Y.map (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.δ F (𝟙_ _) (𝟙_ _))
1444	      = a_Y.map (F.map (λ_ (𝟙_ _)).hom) ≫
1445	        inv (a_Y.map (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.η F ▷ F.obj (𝟙_ _) ≫ (λ_ (F.obj (𝟙_ _))).hom)) := by
1446	    rw [← key, Category.assoc, IsIso.hom_inv_id, Category.comp_id]
1447	  rw [heq]; infer_instance

244	This is the \emph{template} for the proof technique used in route~(e), but it does
245	\emph{not} apply directly to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\): modules over a
246	\emph{varying} ring are not of the form \(C^{op} \to A\) for a fixed monoidal
247	\(A\).
248	
249	\paragraph{The gap and route~(e), superseded.} The substrate
250	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) of this chapter is the
251	sheafification of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\), and
252	sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the localization
253	\(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\). Route~(e)
254	proposed to obtain a \emph{full} monoidal structure on
255	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
256	by applying \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) (ii) to the already-monoidal category (i),
257	which would require the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (its two
258	whiskering-stability fields \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}, with load-bearing
259	local-injectivity half \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}). This is
260	\emph{not} how the group law is realized. The group law consumes only the
261	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms, and the associator is built
262	directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through
263	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}); it never invokes
264	the \emph{full} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance or a coherent monoidal category.
265	The \emph{full monoidal-category} route~(e) is therefore off the critical path. The
266	associator does, however, consume a \emph{single} left-whisker existence lemma
267	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed by the d.2 stalk--tensor
268	commutation; so the stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) itself is on the critical path, even
269	though the full coherent monoidal category built from it is not.
270	
271	The stalk-infrastructure analysis that route~(e) turned on is recorded here for
272	reference only, since that route is superseded. The \emph{stalk module structure}
273	is already present in Mathlib: the file
274	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk} supplies, for a topological
275	space \(X\), a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) on \(X\) and a point
276	\(x \in X\), the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure on the stalk
277	\(\mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) of a presheaf of modules \(M\),
278	together with the germ--scalar compatibility \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\); a linearity
279	packaging of the induced stalk map of a morphism was built project-side
280	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, the \emph{linearity half} of ingredient (d.1)).
281	Route~(e) additionally required a (d.1)-\emph{bridge} between the site-level
282	\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on
283	\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
284	\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor; there is
285	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. The full \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent monoidal category that route~(e) would
286	build is not pursued; but the (d.1)-linearity packaging and the (d.2) stalk--tensor
287	commutation are \emph{on} the critical path, since the associator's single open
288	left-whisker obligation \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk} is closed
289	precisely by (d.2) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}). What is superseded is only the
290	\emph{pair}-of-whiskering-fields \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) packaging and the
291	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category, not the stalk infrastructure.
292	
293	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
294	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
295	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
296	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
297	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
298	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
299	independently of any upstream timing.

301	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
302	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
303	
304	\begin{definition}
305	
306	
307	\leanok
308	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
309	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
310	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
311	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
312	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
313	  \[
314	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
315	  \]
316	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
317	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
318	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
319	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
320	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
321	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
322	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
323	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
324	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
325	  canonical compatibility
326	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
327	     \;\cong\;
328	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
329	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
330	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
331	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
332	
333	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
334	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
335	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
336	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
337	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
338	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
339	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
340	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
341	\end{definition}
342	
343	\begin{lemma}
344	
345	
346	\leanok
347	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
348	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
349	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
350	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
351	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
352	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
353	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
354	  \[
355	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
356	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
357	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
358	  \]
359	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
360	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
361	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
362	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
363	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
364	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
365	  \[
366	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
367	    \qquad
368	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
369	  \]
370	  the associator
371	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
372	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
373	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
374	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
375	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
376	\end{lemma}
377	
378	\begin{proof}
379	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
380	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
381	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
382	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
383	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
384	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
385	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
386	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
387	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
388	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
389	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
390	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
391	\end{proof}
392	
393	\begin{remark}
394	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
395	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
396	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
397	        lem:whisker_of_W}
398	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
399	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
400	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
401	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
402	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
403	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
404	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
405	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
406	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
407	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
408	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
409	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
410	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
411	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
412	
413	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
414	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
415	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
416	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
417	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
418	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
419	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
420	  fixed-base presheaves in
421	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
422	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
423	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
424	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). The \emph{full}
425	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance and the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal
426	  category are not on the critical path: the group law is realized without them. The
427	  single left-whisker existence lemma and its underlying d.1/d.2 stalk apparatus, by
428	  contrast, \emph{are} on the critical path, since the associator's sheafification
429	  transport consumes exactly that one whisker obligation
430	  (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed via d.2).
431	
432	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
433	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
434	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
435	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
436	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
437	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
438	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
439	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
440	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
441	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
442	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
443	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
444	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
445	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
446	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
447	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
448	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
449	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
450	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
451	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
452	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
453	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
454	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
455	\end{remark}
456	
457	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
458	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
459	
460	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
461	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
462	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
463	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
464	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
465	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
466	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
467	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
468	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
469	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
470	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
471	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
472	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
473	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
474	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
475	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
476	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
477	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
478	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
479	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
480	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
481	to the off-path route~(e).

873	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
874	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
875	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
876	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
877	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
878	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
879	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
880	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
881	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
882	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
883	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
884	\end{lemma}
885	
886	\begin{proof}
887	
888	
889	
890	
891	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
892	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
893	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
894	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
895	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
896	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
897	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
898	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
899	\end{proof}

914	\begin{lemma}
915	% Closed, sorry-free standalone result (flat whiskering). Off the critical path:
916	% the associator is realized via the d.2 stalk-tensor route
917	% (lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk), not via this flat lemma.

919	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
920	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
921	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
922	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
923	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
924	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
925	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
926	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
927	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
928	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
929	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
930	  left-whiskered morphism
931	  \[
932	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
933	  \]
934	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
935	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
936	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
937	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
938	
939	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
940	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
941	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
942	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability obligation
943	  for the unconditional associator is closed for an \emph{arbitrary} whiskering
944	  object \(F\) by the stalk--tensor commutation
945	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
946	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}), which never invokes the
947	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness
948	  condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the
949	  site, including non-affine opens --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility
950	  or local triviality, which are affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma
951	  is therefore retained as an independent, closed result, superseded on the critical
952	  path by the d.2 route of \cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}.
953	\end{lemma}
954	
955	\begin{proof}
956	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
957	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
958	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
959	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
960	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
961	
962	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
963	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
964	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
965	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
966	  \[
967	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
968	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
969	  \]
970	
971	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
972	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
973	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
974	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
975	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
976	
977	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
978	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
979	  linear maps (Mathlib
980	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
981	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
982	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
983	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
984	
985	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
986	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
987	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
988	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
989	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
990	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
991	\end{proof}

1240	  \end{itemize}
1241	  Composing, \((F \triangleleft g)|_V\) is isomorphic to \(g|_V\). Now \(g \in J.W\),
1242	  and membership in \(J.W\) (local bijectivity) is stable under restriction along an
1243	  open immersion, so \(g|_V \in J.W\); in particular \(g|_V\) is \(J\)-locally
1244	  injective. An isomorphism preserves local injectivity, hence \((F \triangleleft
1245	  g)|_V\) is locally injective on \(V\). As the \(\{V\}\) cover \(X\) and local
1246	  injectivity is local on the cover, \(F \triangleleft g\) is \(J\)-locally
1247	  injective. \emph{No stalks and no (d.2) stalk--tensor commutation enter}; the
1248	  single comparison lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (a presheaf-level
1249	  comparison, decomposed into its two open-immersion base-change halves) carries the
1250	  argument, and thereby moves \emph{onto} the critical path.
1251	
1252	  This is \emph{not} the section-level route that tensors a bare section map \(g(V)\)
1253	  --- only injective --- and stalls on a \(\mathrm{Tor}_1\)/flatness obligation. Here
1254	  the argument is sheaf-level and uses that \(g\) is locally \emph{bijective} (a
1255	  \(J.W\) morphism), reduced through the unitor to \(g|_V\) (again a \(J.W\)
1256	  morphism); a mere injection is never tensored, so no flatness is needed.
1257	
1258	  \emph{FALLBACK route (arbitrary \(F\); stalkwise, required only for the
1259	  full-generality route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path).} For an
1260	  \emph{arbitrary} (not necessarily locally trivial) \(F\), local injectivity is
1261	  proved stalkwise, by the argument Mathlib uses for fixed-base presheaves in
1262	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\)
1263	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}), ported to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). A
1264	  morphism in \(J.W\) is, on the topological site of \(X\), a \emph{stalkwise
1265	  isomorphism}: at each point \(x\) the induced map on stalks \(g_x\) is an
1266	  isomorphism. The presheaf-of-modules tensor is sectionwise, so it commutes with
1267	  the stalk (a filtered colimit), giving
1268	  \[
1269	    (F \triangleleft g)_x
1270	      \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes_{R_x} g_x
1271	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \;\longrightarrow\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .
1272	  \]
1273	  Tensoring an isomorphism \(g_x\) by the identity \(\mathrm{id}_{F_x}\) yields an
1274	  isomorphism --- no flatness is needed, since \(g_x\) is invertible, not merely
1275	  injective. Hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, in particular
1276	  locally bijective, in particular locally injective, for \emph{any} \(F\).
1277	
1278	  The stalkwise computation rests on three ingredients with distinct status at the
1279	  pinned commit:
1280	  \begin{enumerate}
1281	    \item[(d.1-done)] the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear stalk-map packaging
1282	      (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) and its companions, \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}):
1283	      a morphism of presheaves of modules induces an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear
1284	      map on stalks, characterised on germs, that becomes a linear equivalence once
1285	      the underlying \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map is an isomorphism. This is built
1286	      project-side, axiom-clean, and furnishes the \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step
1287	      above.
1288	    \item[(d.1-bridge)] (remaining) the site-\(J.W\) \(\Longleftrightarrow\)
1289	      stalkwise-iso characterisation on \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\): that a morphism lies
1290	      in \(J.W\) iff its stalk maps are isomorphisms at every point. The concrete
1291	      Mathlib assembly route uses the \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) structure
1292	      together with the topological stalk criteria --- local injectivity
1293	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) injectivity on stalks and local surjectivity
1294	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) surjectivity on stalks --- bridging the site-level
1295	      \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsLocallyInjective}\) / \(\mathtt{IsLocallySurjective}\)
1296	      to the \(\mathtt{TopCat.Presheaf}\) stalk versions. This half is still
1297	      Mathlib-absent at the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
1298	    \item[(d.2)] (remaining) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
1299	      \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk (a filtered colimit) with the relative
1300	      module-presheaf tensor, under which \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) is identified
1301	      with \(\mathrm{lTensor}\,F_x\,(g_x) = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) (using
1302	      that \(\mathtt{tensorLeft}\) / \(\mathtt{tensorRight}\) preserve filtered
1303	      colimits over a module category). This is genuinely Mathlib-absent over a
1304	      \emph{varying} ring and is the largest remaining piece.
1305	  \end{enumerate}
1306	  Mathlib supplies the analogues for presheaves valued in a fixed monoidal concrete
1307	  category, but not for modules over a varying ring; porting (d.1-bridge) and (d.2)
1308	  to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) is the remaining work. Once (d.2) lands, the
1309	  conclusion is immediate and flatness-free: by
1310	  \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\) the stalk map \(g_x\) is a
1311	  linear equivalence, and the linear-equivalence \(\mathrm{lTensor}\) by \(F_x\)
1312	  carries it to an isomorphism, finishing the proof.
1313	
1314	  This stalkwise fallback is required \emph{only} if the full-generality
1315	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) route~(e) --- the
1316	  monoidal category on \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), for an
1317	  arbitrary whisker object \(F\) --- is later wanted. For the relative Picard group
1318	  law the whisker objects are locally trivial, and the PRIMARY route above suffices,
1319	  trading the Mathlib-absent (d.2) for the single comparison
1320	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
1321	\end{proof}

1324	supplies the two whiskering-stability fields of the abandoned
1325	\((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}). The group law
1326	needs no such instance: the associator is built directly by gluing canonical local
1327	isomorphisms (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).
1328	This block is retained for the historical record only and must \emph{not} be
1329	formalized.

1331	\begin{lemma}
1332	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.
1333	[Flatness-free whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
1334	  \label{lem:whisker_of_W}
1335	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1336	  With notation as in \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}: for an
1337	  \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of \(R\)-modules \(F\) and a morphism \(g\) whose
1338	  underlying additive-presheaf map lies in \(J.W\), both whiskerings again lie in
1339	  \(J.W\):
1340	  \[
1341	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; F \triangleleft g \in J.W,
1342	    \qquad
1343	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; g \triangleright F \in J.W .
1344	  \]
1345	  These two statements are precisely the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) and
1346	  \(\mathtt{whiskerRight}\) data required by the class
1347	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) for
1348	  the morphism property \(J.W\); together they furnish the instance
1349	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) of \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}. No flatness and no
1350	  local triviality is used --- they are the flatness-free replacements for the
1351	  flat variants of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}.
1352	\end{lemma}
1353	
1354	\begin{proof}
1355	  \uses{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1356	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of \(J.W\), it suffices to show
1357	  \(F \triangleleft g\) is both locally injective and locally surjective.
1358	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is free and needs no hypothesis on \(F\): the presheaf
1359	  tensor is right exact, so \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the locally-zero cokernel
1360	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\), which is therefore locally
1361	  zero (Mathlib \(\mathtt{isLocallySurjective\_whiskerLeft}\)). \emph{Local
1362	  injectivity} is \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. The two together
1363	  give \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
1364	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
1365	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf monoidal structure, which is an isomorphism
1366	  and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\).
1367	\end{proof}

1371	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) from \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\). It is not
1372	needed. The group law consumes only the existence of the associator, unitor and
1373	braiding isomorphisms (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, assembled by hand

1379	\begin{lemma}
1380	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.
1381	[The \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal substrate (route~(e) target)]
1382	  \label{lem:jw_ismonoidal}
1383	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:whisker_of_W}
1384	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The sheafification localizer \(J.W\) on
1385	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) is \emph{monoidal}: it carries an
1386	  instance
1387	  \[
1388	    J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal},
1389	  \]
1390	  whose two fields are the whiskering-stability lemmas \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}
1391	  (and which is multiplicative because sheafification inverts identities and
1392	  composites). Consequently, by Mathlib's
1393	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)
1394	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) applied to the already-monoidal category
1395	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\), the localization functor
1396	  \(L = \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\) (which is the localization at
1397	  \(J.W\)), and the unit isomorphism \(L(\mathbf{1}) \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), the
1398	  category
1399	  \[
1400	    \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
1401	      \;=\; \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
1402	  \]
1403	  acquires a full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure whose tensor is the substrate
1404	  \(\otimes_X\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}) and whose associator,
1405	  left/right unitors, braiding and \emph{all} coherence (pentagon, triangle,
1406	  naturalities) are derived by the API. This is the route~(e) target: the
1407	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors
1408	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso} are
1409	  then components of this derived monoidal structure rather than hand-assembled
1410	  composites.
1411	
1412	  This block states the route~(e) construction; it depends on the sole open obligation
1413	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. It mirrors Mathlib's
1414	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) construction one categorical level up.
1415	\end{lemma}
1416	
1417	\begin{proof}
1418	  \uses{lem:whisker_of_W}
1419	  Multiplicativity of \(J.W\) (closure under identities and composition) is part
1420	  of its being a localizer (\(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). The two
1421	  whiskering-stability fields of \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) are
1422	  exactly the left- and right-whiskering statements of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
1423	  Hence \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) holds. Sheafification of presheaves of
1424	  modules is a localization at \(J.W\) (the underlying identification of
1425	  sheafification with localization at the locally-bijective class), and the unit
1426	  presheaf sheafifies to \(\mathcal{O}_X\); feeding these to
1427	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) produces the \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
1428	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with all coherence derived, as in
1429	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}. No \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure enters
1430	  at any point.
1431	\end{proof}

1433	\begin{lemma}
1434	
1435	
1436	\leanok
1437	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ (unconditional)]
1438	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
1439	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso}
1440	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,
1441	        lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
1442	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
1443	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). There is an isomorphism
1444	  \[
1445	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
1446	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1447	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
1448	  \]
1449	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), natural in \(M, N, P\). The group law on
1450	  \(\otimes\)-iso-classes (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) consumes only
1451	  the \emph{existence} of this isomorphism, the proposition
1452	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \cong M \otimes_X (N
1453	  \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality coherence of it is
1454	  ever required.
1455	
1456	  The Lean pin is unconditional: it holds for arbitrary
1457	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(M, N, P\), with no local-triviality or
1458	  invertibility hypothesis. (Earlier drafts carried vestigial
1459	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) hypotheses to match the carrier; these were
1460	  dropped once the construction below was seen to use none of them.)
1461	\end{lemma}
1462	
1463	\begin{proof}
1464	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,
1465	        lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
1466	  \textbf{Realization (sheafification transport, unconditional via d.2).} The
1467	  associator is the sheafification transport of the presheaf-of-modules associator
1468	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\), which exists for
1469	  \emph{all} presheaves of modules over the varying ring. The transport requires the
1470	  single left-whisker \(F \triangleleft \mathtt{toSheafify}\) to lie in the
1471	  sheafification localizer \(J.W\) for the relevant \(F\); that single open
1472	  left-whiskering obligation \(\mathtt{isLocallyInjective\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) is
1473	  closed \emph{unconditionally} --- for an arbitrary whiskering object, with no
1474	  flatness and no local triviality --- by the d.2 stalk--tensor commutation
1475	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk} (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}):
1476	  a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, and by the d.2 commutation
1477	  \(\bigl(F \triangleleft g\bigr)_x \cong \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) is again an
1478	  isomorphism, so \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). Hence the associator is itself
1479	  unconditional and axiom-clean once d.2 (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}) is
1480	  formalized; it does \emph{not} require the full \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
1481	  or the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category --- only the one left-whisker
1482	  lemma --- and no sectionwise flatness enters. The local-gluing description below
1483	  records the equivalent concrete local realization through the
1484	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
1485	
1486	  Concretely, the same isomorphism is described by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
1487	  through the restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
1488	  Fix a
1489	  common open cover \(\{U\}\) of \(X\). On each member \(U\) form the canonical
1490	  composite
1491	  \[
1492	    \bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P\bigr)\big|_U
1493	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1494	    \bigl(M|_U \otimes_U N|_U\bigr) \otimes_U P|_U
1495	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1496	    M|_U \otimes_U \bigl(N|_U \otimes_U P|_U\bigr)
1497	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1498	    \bigl(M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\big|_U ,
1499	  \]
1500	  in which:
1501	  \begin{itemize}
1502	    \item the first arrow is two applications of the restriction-compatibility
1503	      isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} along the open immersion
1504	      \(U \hookrightarrow X\): first \((M \otimes_X N)|_U \cong M|_U \otimes_U
1505	      N|_U\) tensored with \(P|_U\), then \(\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X
1506	      P\bigr)|_U \cong (M \otimes_X N)|_U \otimes_U P|_U\);
1507	    \item the middle arrow is the \emph{canonical presheaf-level associator}
1508	      \(\alpha\) of the (symmetric) monoidal category
1509	      \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\)
1510	      (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\),
1511	      \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)), read on the restricted modules;
1512	    \item the third arrow is again two applications of
1513	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, in the opposite direction.
1514	  \end{itemize}
1515	  Each of the three arrows is a \emph{canonical} isomorphism: the
1516	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is induced
1517	  by the open immersion with no choices, hence natural in its module arguments, and
1518	  the presheaf associator \(\alpha\) is a fixed natural transformation. Therefore,
1519	  on a double overlap \(U \cap U'\), restricting the \(U\)-composite and the
1520	  \(U'\)-composite to \(U \cap U'\) yields the same morphism: each of the three
1521	  arrows commutes with the further restriction to \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1522	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the presheaf associator, so the two local
1523	  composites agree on the overlap.
1524	
1525	  Because the internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(-,-)\) of sheaves of
1526	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is itself a sheaf on \(X\), a family of morphisms defined
1527	  on the members of an open cover and agreeing on the overlaps glues to a single
1528	  global morphism. Applying this both to the local composites above and to their
1529	  local inverses produces a global morphism
1530	  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \to M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\) whose restriction
1531	  to each \(U\) is the local composite, together with a two-sided inverse glued from
1532	  the local inverses; hence the glued morphism is a global isomorphism --- the
1533	  associator. The single non-formal input of this re-route is the
1534	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the presheaf
1535	  associator (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)) and the sheaf-of-modules
1536	  internal-hom gluing are already available.
1537	
1538	  Note that, unlike the pointwise-existence statement
1539	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} --- a \emph{proposition} proved
1540	  cover-pointwise with \emph{no} overlap obligation --- the associator is
1541	  \emph{global data}, a single isomorphism of sheaves on all of \(X\). Assembling it
1542	  from local pieces is therefore genuinely stronger than that pattern: the
1543	  overlap-naturality compatibility of the local composites must be discharged before
1544	  gluing, and is discharged above. The locally-trivial hypotheses of the statement
1545	  are not consumed; the comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is applied to the
1546	  global, untrivialised restrictions \((M \otimes_X N)|_U\), so no free-cover
1547	  specialisation can replace it. What this proof does \emph{not} require is the
1548	  \emph{full} \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance or the
1549	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category
1550	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W,lem:jw_ismonoidal}): only the single left-whisker obligation
1551	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed via the d.2 stalk--tensor
1552	  commutation \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}, is consumed by the sheafification
1553	  transport. The group law on \(\otimes\)-iso-classes
1554	  consumes only the \emph{existence} of the resulting isomorphism, never its pentagon,
1555	  triangle, or naturality coherence.
1556	\end{proof}

1558	\begin{lemma}
1559	[Left and right unitors for $\otimes_X$]
1560	  \label{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1561	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_left_unitor, AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_right_unitor}
1562	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}

1577	  the left and right unitors \(\lambda, \rho\) at the presheaf level. Their

1614	\leanok
1615	[Inverse of an invertible module]

1843	\section{The varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation (d.2) and the unconditional
1844	left-whiskering}
1845	\label{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}
1846	
1847	This section supplies the one genuinely Mathlib-absent ingredient on which the
1848	\emph{unconditional} associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} rests: the commutation
1849	of the stalk functor with the relative-ring tensor product of presheaves of modules
1850	(``d.2''). Together with the already-built \(R_x\)-linear stalk comparison
1851	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, ``d.1'') it closes the single open left-whiskering
1852	obligation for an \emph{arbitrary} whiskering object --- with no flatness and no
1853	local-triviality hypothesis --- which is exactly what makes the sheafification
1854	transport of the presheaf associator work for all modules. The construction is to be
1855	formalized in the dedicated Lean file
1856	\(\mathtt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/StalkTensor.lean}\).
1857	
1858	\begin{lemma}
1859	
1860	\leanok
1861	[Stalk of a tensor product (d.2): varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation]
1862	  \label{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}
1863	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorIso}
1864	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map}
1865	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Tensor product),
1866	  % Lemma lemma-stalk-tensor-product (stalk of a tensor product of modules is the
1867	  % tensor product of the stalks over the stalk ring)
1868	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2332-L2344).
1869	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1870	  % "\begin{lemma}
1871	  % \label{lemma-stalk-tensor-product}
1872	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1873	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
1874	  % Let $x \in X$. There is a canonical isomorphism
1875	  % of $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$-modules
1876	  % $$
1877	  % (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})_x
1878	  % =
1879	  % \mathcal{F}_x \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{X, x}} \mathcal{G}_x
1880	  % $$
1881	  % functorial in $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$.
1882	  % \end{lemma}"
1883	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces,
1884	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-stalk-tensor-product}.}
1885	  Let \(R : (\mathtt{Opens}\,X)^{op} \to \mathtt{CommRingCat}\) be the structure
1886	  presheaf of a scheme \(X\), and let \(A, B\) be presheaves of
1887	  \(R\)-modules (objects of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,(R \circ
1888	  \mathtt{forget_2}\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat})\)). For each point
1889	  \(x \in X\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \((R \circ
1890	  \mathtt{forget_2}).\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-modules
1891	  \[
1892	    (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
1893	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1894	    A.\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\otimes_{R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x}\; B.\mathtt{stalk}\,x,
1895	  \]
1896	  natural in \(A\) and \(B\), where \(\otimes^{p}\) is the presheaf-of-modules tensor
1897	  product \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\).
1898	\end{lemma}
1899	
1900	\begin{proof}
1901	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map}
1902	  \leanok
1903	  The stalk at \(x\) is the filtered colimit over the neighbourhoods \(U \ni x\) of
1904	  the sections functor, and sectionwise the tensor product is computed pointwise:
1905	  \((A \otimes^{p} B)(U) = A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\)
1906	  (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj\_obj}\)). The neighbourhood system
1907	  \(\{U \ni x\}\) is cofiltered under inclusion, so the colimit is filtered. The
1908	  isomorphism is assembled in five named stages. Stages (i)--(ii) construct the
1909	  forward additive comparison map and its germ characterisation; stages (iii)--(v)
1910	  upgrade it to an \(R_x\)-linear map, construct the reverse map, and check mutual
1911	  inversion.
1912	
1913	  \emph{(i) Per-neighbourhood balanced bilinear map and its section-level descent}
1914	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorBilin}\), \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}\)). For a fixed
1915	  neighbourhood \(U \ni x\) the assignment
1916	  \[
1917	    (a, b) \;\longmapsto\; \mathrm{germ}_x\,a \,\otimes\, \mathrm{germ}_x\,b,
1918	    \qquad a \in A(U),\ b \in B(U),
1919	  \]
1920	  is \(R(U)\)-balanced bilinear into \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\): it is additive in each
1921	  variable, and for \(r \in R(U)\) the two ways of moving \(r\) across the tensor agree
1922	  after passing to germs, since \(\mathrm{germ}_x(r \cdot a) = (\mathrm{germ}_x r)
1923	  \cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a\) and the stalk tensor is balanced over
1924	  \(R_x = R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) (the germ--scalar compatibility of
1925	  \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}). By the universal property of the relative tensor
1926	  product it descends to an additive map on the section module
1927	  \[
1928	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}_U \;\colon\;
1929	      (A \otimes^{p} B)(U) = A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)
1930	        \;\longrightarrow\; A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x,
1931	    \qquad a \otimes b \longmapsto \mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x b.
1932	  \]
1933	
1934	  \emph{(ii) Colimit descent to the forward comparison map}
1935	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\), \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)). The
1936	  maps \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}_U\) are compatible with the restriction maps of the
1937	  presheaf \(A \otimes^{p} B\) as \(U\) shrinks toward \(x\) (a smaller neighbourhood
1938	  computes the same germs), so they form a cocone over the filtered diagram
1939	  \(\{(A \otimes^{p} B)(U)\}_{U \ni x}\). The induced map out of the colimit is the
1940	  forward additive comparison map
1941	  \[
1942	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc} \;\colon\;
1943	      (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\longrightarrow\; A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x,
1944	  \]
1945	  characterised on germs of simple tensors by
1946	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\bigl(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes b)\bigr) =
1947	  \mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x b\)
1948	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)). This is the forward half of the asserted
1949	  isomorphism (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_desc_forward}).
1950	
1951	  \emph{(iii) \(R_x\)-linearity packaging} (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap}\)). The
1952	  additive map of (ii) is upgraded to an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear map: on a germ
1953	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x r\) of a section \(r \in R(U)\) one checks
1954	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\bigl((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \xi\bigr) =
1955	  (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}(\xi)\) on germs of simple tensors
1956	  and extends by additivity. The one subtlety is a \emph{carrier-duality obstacle}:
1957	  the section-level tensor \(A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\) is a module over the
1958	  \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier \((R \circ \mathtt{forget_2})(U)\), whereas the natural
1959	  scalar \(r : R(U)\) lives in the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) carrier \(R(U)\); the two
1960	  carriers are canonically identified but not definitionally equal, so the
1961	  scalar-pulling lemma \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.smul\_tmul'}\) applies only after
1962	  inserting a canonical \(\mathtt{RingEquiv}\) bridge between the
1963	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carriers. Once that bridge is
1964	  in place the linearity computation mirrors the germ-representative pattern used
1965	  for the d.1 stalk map \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) (\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}).
1966	
1967	  \emph{(iv) The reverse map.} The map
1968	  \[
1969	    A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \;\longrightarrow\; (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
1970	  \]
1971	  is built by the universal property of the tensor product over \(R_x\) from the
1972	  \(R_x\)-bilinear map of the two stalks
1973	  \[
1974	    (\mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b) \;\longmapsto\;
1975	      \mathrm{germ}_x\bigl(a|_{U \cap V} \otimes b|_{U \cap V}\bigr),
1976	  \]
1977	  where \(a \in A(U)\) and \(b \in B(V)\) are chosen representatives; this is a
1978	  \emph{nested} colimit descent, descending first the \(A\)-variable out of the
1979	  filtered colimit \(\{A(U)\}_{U \ni x}\) and then the \(B\)-variable out of
1980	  \(\{B(V)\}_{V \ni x}\), passing to the common refinement \(U \cap V\) to form the
1981	  simple tensor and its germ. Well-definedness on germs (independence of the chosen
1982	  representatives and of the neighbourhoods \(U, V\)) is exactly filteredness of the
1983	  neighbourhood system.
1984	
1985	  It remains to check that the reverse map is \(R_x\)-bilinear, and in particular that
1986	  its underlying bilinear map is \emph{balanced} over \(R_x\), i.e. that pulling a scalar
1987	  out of the left variable agrees with pulling it out of the right. The key point is to
1988	  establish this balancing \emph{at the stalk level}, with every scalar living in the
1989	  stalk ring \(R_x = R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) throughout. Concretely, for a germ
1990	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x r\) of a section \(r \in R(W)\) one verifies the identity
1991	  \[
1992	    \mathtt{revBihom}\bigl((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b\bigr)
1993	      \;=\; (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathtt{revBihom}\bigl(\mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b\bigr),
1994	  \]
1995	  and symmetrically on the right variable, where the scalar \(\mathrm{germ}_x r \in R_x\)
1996	  never leaves the stalk ring. This holds because of the germ--scalar compatibility
1997	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x(r \cdot s) = (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot(\mathrm{germ}_x s)\) of the germ
1998	  map \(R(W) \to R_x\) (the Lean lemma \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\)) — the same germ--scalar
1999	  compatibility already used in stages (i) and (iii). Multiplying a representative
2000	  \(r \cdot a\) and then taking germs produces \((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a\),
2001	  so the action of \(r\) is transparently the \(R_x\)-action on the stalk and may be
2002	  moved freely across the tensor of stalks, where balancedness over \(R_x\) is built into
2003	  \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\). Because the whole computation takes place over the single
2004	  ring \(R_x\), no carrier identification intervenes and the balancing follows directly.
2005	
2006	  One must \emph{not} instead reduce this balancing to a section-level identity. If the
2007	  scalar were pushed through the presheaf restriction to a common neighbourhood \(W\) and
2008	  the balancing read off inside the section module \(A(W) \otimes_{R(W)} B(W)\), then the
2009	  scalar action appearing there is the one produced by the presheaf-of-modules structure
2010	  of \(A\) via restriction of scalars: it lives over the \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier
2011	  \((R \circ \mathtt{forget_2})(W)\), \emph{not} over the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) carrier
2012	  \(R(W)\) that annotates the section tensor. This is precisely the
2013	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-vs-\(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier-duality obstacle already met in
2014	  stage (iii), here compounded by the extra restriction-of-scalars wrapper, so the naive
2015	  ``move the scalar across the tensor'' step (\(\mathtt{TensorProduct.smul\_tmul'}\)) does
2016	  not apply at the section level without first inserting the carrier bridge. Working at the
2017	  stalk level as above sidesteps this wrapper entirely.
2018	
2019	  Where a \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-vs-\(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier identification is
2020	  genuinely unavoidable, it is the \emph{same} canonical bridge already invoked for the
2021	  stage-(iii) \(R_x\)-linearity packaging (the \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU\_smul}\)/%
2022	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap}\) step, \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_linear_map}): the two
2023	  carriers are canonically identified but not definitionally equal, and the same
2024	  \(\mathtt{RingEquiv}\) bridge resolves the obstruction here. Thus the stage-(iv)
2025	  balancing reuses the technique already resolved in stage (iii) rather than re-deriving
2026	  it.
2027	
2028	  \emph{(v) Mutual inversion on germ generators} (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\)). The two
2029	  composites are checked to be identities on generators. The composite
2030	  \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \to (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \to A_x
2031	  \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) is the identity on a simple tensor \(\mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes
2032	  \mathrm{germ}_x b\) by the germ characterisation \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)
2033	  of (ii), and extends to all of \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) by
2034	  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.induction\_on}\). The opposite composite
2035	  \((A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \to A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \to (A \otimes^{p}
2036	  B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) is the identity on a germ \(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes b)\) of
2037	  a simple tensor by the same characterisation; since germs generate the stalk and the
2038	  germ maps are jointly epimorphic (\(\mathtt{stalk\_hom\_ext}\)), together with
2039	  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.induction\_on}\) applied to each section, the composite is the
2040	  identity. Bundling the \(R_x\)-linear forward map of (iii) with the reverse map of
2041	  (iv) and these two inversion identities yields the asserted isomorphism
2042	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\).
2043	
2044	  Naturality in \(A, B\) is inherited from naturality of the sectionwise tensor and of
2045	  the germ maps. This is the presheaf-of-modules, varying-ring analogue of the Stacks
2046	  lemma above; the only subtlety beyond the ringed-space statement is that the ground
2047	  ring varies with \(U\), handled by the ``tensor of filtered colimits over the colimit
2048	  ring'' identification together with the carrier-duality bridge of (iii). Conceptually
2049	  the tensor product of modules commutes with filtered colimits in each variable and
2050	  the ground ring \(R(U)\) itself colimits to the stalk ring \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\),
2051	  which is the reason the forward map (ii) and the reverse map (iv) are mutually
2052	  inverse.
2053	\end{proof}

2139	  local bijectivity: \(g\) is both \(J\)-locally injective and \(J\)-locally
2140	  surjective. On the topological site \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) the Grothendieck-topology
2141	  notions of local injectivity and local surjectivity coincide with the classical
2142	  ``locally on a cover'' conditions, and these are detected stalkwise: a morphism of
2143	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-valued presheaves is locally injective iff every stalk map is
2144	  injective and locally surjective iff every stalk map is surjective. Concretely, for

2348	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), and
2349	  then \(\mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
2350	  \mathcal{O}_X)\).}
2351	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). We say \(M\)
2352	  is \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertible} when it admits a tensor inverse: there is an
2353	  object \(N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) together with an isomorphism
2354	  \[
2355	    M \otimes_X N \;\cong\;
2356	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
2357	      \;=\; \mathcal{O}_X ,
2358	  \]
2359	  i.e.\ \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M) :\equiv \exists\, N,\
2360	  \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong
2361	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)\), with
2362	  \(\otimes_X\) the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}. The
2363	  predicate is a \emph{proposition} carrying its inverse witness existentially;
2364	  consequently the dual/inverse needed by the group law is definitional and no
2365	  separate ``a tensor inverse exists'' lemma is consumed downstream. This is the
2366	  scheme-level analogue of Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\), transported
2367	  one categorical level up to \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with the substrate
2368	  \(\otimes_X\).
2369	\end{definition}

2371	\begin{lemma}[The commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes and its units]
2372	  \label{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}

2423	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces,
2424	  ``Invertible modules''), Definition~01CX and Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-%
2425	  invertible}. The isomorphism classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
2426	  form an abelian group \(\Pic(X)\) under \(\otimes\), with unit
2427	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and inverse the dual; the tensor product of invertibles is
2428	  invertible and the dual is a tensor inverse.}
2429	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
2430	  objects of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
2431	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) form a commutative monoid under the
2432	  substrate tensor product \(\otimes_X\) in which every element is a unit ---
2433	  equivalently, an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag
2434	  01CR).
2435	
2436	  \textbf{Primary construction (directly on locally-trivial iso-classes, à la
2437	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\)).} The commutative group is built directly on
2438	  isomorphism classes of \emph{locally-trivial} (equivalently \(\otimes\)-invertible)
2439	  sheaves, mirroring Mathlib's ring-level Picard group
2440	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
2441	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The group operation is
2442	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\), well-defined on iso-classes by the
2443	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
2444	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) and again locally trivial by
2445	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}; the identity is
2446	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and the inverse of \([L]\) is the class of the dual
2447	  \([L^{-1}]\) supplied by \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (with
2448	  \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)).
2449	
2450	  The four group-axiom fields are defined \emph{by hand}, each discharged by a
2451	  \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism read as an equality of iso-classes --- passage
2452	  to iso-classes turns an isomorphism of sheaves into an equality of classes:
2453	  \[
2454	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \lambda_X \rangle,
2455	    \quad
2456	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \rho_X \rangle,
2457	    \quad
2458	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \alpha_{X,Y,Z} \rangle,
2459	    \quad
2460	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \beta_{X,Y} \rangle,
2461	  \]
2462	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity is
2463	  the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
2464	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, each consumed only as the proposition
2465	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\); the pentagon, triangle and hexagon
2466	  coherence carried by a \(\MonoidalCategory\) typeclass is \emph{never} invoked in
2467	  any of the four axiom proofs.
2468	
2469	  This by-hand assembly does \emph{not} go through
2470	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)
2471	  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}): those constructions
2472	  \emph{require} a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\) (and, for the commutative
2473	  law, \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on the carrier category --- precisely the
2474	  coherent monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) over the \emph{varying}
2475	  structure sheaf that this construction explicitly avoids. Consequently the group law
2476	  requires \emph{no} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \emph{no}
2477	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\) instance on
2478	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The genuine precedent is Mathlib's fixed-ring
2479	  Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
2480	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}):
2481	  there the \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) \emph{is} read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the
2482	  coherent monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\)
2483	  that Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring. The present construction \emph{cannot} take
2484	  that \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route --- no coherent monoidal category on
2485	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is available --- so
2486	  its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is built directly from the existence-of-isomorphism
2487	  propositions above, the construction ultimately resting, as
2488	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) does, on iso-classes of invertible modules. The pinned
2489	  carrier \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}\) realizes this commutative-group
2490	  structure; the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at the
2491	  pinned commit.
2492	
2493	  This is the \emph{lower-risk} path: every ingredient is either already proved
2494	  (the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, the braiding
2495	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}) or locally-trivial-scoped and assembled (the
2496	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}). The only remaining obligations on this
2497	  critical path are the restriction-compatibility comparison
2498	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and the
2499	  dual/inverse \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. The associator
2500	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} is supplied by the unconditional route of
2501	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor} and transitively invokes the d.2 stalk--tensor
2502	  commutation \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}.
2503	
2504	  \textbf{Fallback (full route~(e)).} Alternatively, once
2505	  \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} lands, \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X =
2506	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is a full (symmetric)
2507	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) via \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\),
2508	  and the Picard group is the units of its skeleton,
2509	  \(\Pic(X) = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\),
2510	  exactly as Mathlib builds \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R =
2511	  \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\) and transports
2512	  \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\). This fallback yields the group on \emph{all}
2513	  invertible modules and is retained for the full monoidal generality, but it is
2514	  not required for the relative Picard group law, which the primary construction
2515	  already supplies.
2516	\end{lemma}

2524	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
2525	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The binary operation
2526	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\) is well-defined on iso-classes by the
2527	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
2528	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) --- an isomorphism in either
2529	  argument tensors to an isomorphism --- and its value is again locally trivial by
2530	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, so the operation closes on
2531	  the carrier. The identity element is \([\mathcal{O}_X]\). The four group-axiom
2532	  fields are each defined \emph{by hand} from a \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism,
2533	  read as an equality of iso-classes,

2535	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \leftarrow \langle\alpha\rangle,
2536	    \quad
2537	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \leftarrow \langle\lambda\rangle,
2538	    \quad
2539	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \leftarrow \langle\rho\rangle,
2540	    \quad
